1: This is the law (torah) of the guilt offering (asham). It is most holy — the guilt offering rubric opens ch 7; the most-holy classification places the asham alongside the sin offering and grain offering as restricted to the sanctuary court and male priests. The asham legislation continues from 5:14–6:7, but ch 7 provides the priestly service details (as chs 6–7 do for each sacrifice type).
2: In the place where they kill the burnt offering they shall kill the guilt offering, and its blood shall be thrown against the sides of the altar all around — the slaughter location (north side, same as burnt offering and sin offering) unifies the most-holy offerings spatially; the blood manipulation for the asham is the zeriqah (throwing/dashing against altar sides) rather than the netinah (applying with finger to altar horns) used for the sin offering. The difference marks the asham as a reparation/restoration offering rather than an atonement-covering offering.
3: And all its fat shall be offered, the fat tail, the fat that covers the entrails — the fat portions required of the asham are identical to those of the peace offering (Lev 3:3-5,9-10,14-15): tail, covering fat, kidneys, and liver lobe. All fat belongs to YHWH (Lev 3:16) regardless of sacrifice type.
4: The two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys — the kidney-liver complex that functions as the presumed seat of intellect and emotion in Hebrew anthropology is dedicated to YHWH in every sacrificial animal; the offerer never retains access to these organs. Mesopotamian extispicy (divination from animal entrails) focused on precisely these organs (liver, lungs) as windows to divine will — Levitical legislation redirects them entirely to YHWH's altar, closing them off as sources of illicit divine knowledge.
5: The priest shall burn them on the altar as a food offering to YHWH; it is a guilt offering — the burning of the fat portions as isheh (food offering by fire) is the YHWH-dedicated portion of the asham, as it is for the burnt and peace offerings. The asham's structure thus includes a YHWH-portion (fat burned), a priest-portion (meat, vv.6-10), but no offerer-portion (unlike the peace offering).
6: Every male among the priests may eat of it. It shall be eaten in a holy place. It is most holy — asham meat consumption follows sin offering rules (Lev 6:26,29): male priests only, within the sanctuary court. The consistency of most-holy rules across chatat and asham reinforces their shared category as offerings where the priest bears the offerer's guilt through priestly eating.
7: The guilt offering is just like the sin offering; there is one law for them. The priest who makes atonement with it shall have it — the equivalence formula confirms that chatat and asham share the most-holy priestly-eating rule; the priest who performs the specific atoning ritual has a claim on that offering's meat. This personal stake in the offering by the officiating priest reinforces the depth of priestly identification with the atonement act.
8: And the priest who offers any man's burnt offering shall have for himself the skin of the burnt offering that he has offered — the burnt offering skin is the officiating priest's perquisite even though the entire animal is consumed on the altar; the skin (hide) is a valuable commodity. Comparative evidence from Mesopotamian temple records (prebend archives from Nippur and Sippar) shows priests received specific portions of temple income, including animal hides — the Levitical system is consistent with the broader ANE priestly prebend system.
9: And every grain offering baked in an oven and all that is prepared in a pan or on a griddle shall belong to the priest who offers it — the officiating priest keeps the grain offering remainder after burning the azkarah; the method of preparation (oven, pan, griddle) determines the specific priestly ownership claim. The grain offering is explicitly the officiating priest's perquisite, not shared among all the sons of Aaron (contrast the sin offering portions of v.6 where all male priests may eat).
10: But every grain offering, whether mixed with oil or dry, shall be shared equally among all the sons of Aaron — a distinction between prepared grain offerings (v.9, officiating priest's) and raw/dry grain offerings (v.10, shared equally). The equal distribution of certain priestly portions reflects a deliberate policy of communal priestly economic equity; later developments in the priestly rotation system (1 Chron 24) extended this equity to the weekly service assignments themselves.
11: And this is the law of the sacrifice of peace offerings (torat zevach ha-shelamim) that one may offer to YHWH — the peace offering (shelamim) rubric is the most elaborate in the chapter because the shelamim has three subtypes (toda, neder, nadavah) with different time limits and specific firstfruit-bread requirements. The shelamim is also the only sacrifice in which the lay offerer retains a portion of the meat.
12: If he offers it for a thanksgiving (toda), then he shall offer with the thanksgiving sacrifice unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers smeared with oil, and loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil — the toda (thanksgiving) peace offering requires the largest bread accompaniment: three types of unleavened bread plus leavened bread (v.13). The toda is the most festive subcategory; the multiple bread types generate multiple portions distributed to the priests and create a communal meal context.
13: With the sacrifice of his peace offerings for thanksgiving he shall bring his offering with loaves of leavened bread (chal chametz) — leavened bread accompanies the toda (alone among Levitical offerings); the leaven prohibition applies to offerings burned on the altar, but the bread accompanying the toda is not burned. The leavened loaves are presented as a terumah (contribution) and given to the officiating priest (v.14).
14: And from it he shall offer one loaf from each offering, as a contribution (terumah) to YHWH. It shall belong to the priest who throws the blood of the peace offerings — the wave/heave offering mechanism: one bread from each type becomes the priestly perquisite through the terumah presentation. The blood-throwing priest (who performed the animal's sacrificial manipulation) receives the bread portion, again linking the officiating role to specific material perquisites.
15: And the flesh of the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offerings shall be eaten on the day of his offering. He shall not leave any of it until the morning (velo yaniach mimenu ad boqer) — the toda's one-day eating limit is the strictest: it must be consumed on the day of sacrifice, with nothing left overnight. This rule ensures the toda remains a genuine communal celebration rather than a family food store; the immediate consumption requirement mirrors the Passover lamb rule (Exod 12:10) and may reflect similar concerns about sacred meat's vulnerability to contamination.
16: But if the sacrifice of his offering is a vow (neder) or a freewill offering (nadavah), it shall be eaten on the day that he offers his sacrifice, and on the next day what remains of it shall be eaten — vow and freewill peace offerings have a two-day eating window (day of sacrifice + next day), one day more than the toda; the less ceremonially charged context of vow-fulfillment and spontaneous generosity permits the extra day. In the ancient Near East, communal feasting from sacrificed animals was standard practice (Ugaritic dbh texts; Mesopotamian siskur records), but the specific time limits for consumption are a distinctively Israelite regulation.
17: But what remains of the flesh of the sacrifice on the third day shall be burned up with fire — the three-day/two-day rule terminates with mandatory burning of any remaining meat; the third day is the critical boundary. The same three-day framework appears in the tainted offering (pigul) legislation of Lev 19:7-8; third-day meat is considered ritually void regardless of physical condition.
18: If any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offering is eaten on the third day, he who offers it shall not be accepted, neither shall it be credited to him. It shall be tainted meat (pigul yihyeh), and the person who eats of it shall bear his iniquity — eating third-day pigul carries personal guilt (avon) for the eater; the offering becomes not merely valueless but actively harmful to eat. The technical term pigul (abomination, voided offering) here applies to peace offerings consumed out of time, as it applies in Lev 19:7 to the original offeror's invalid intent.
19: Flesh that touches any unclean thing shall not be eaten. It shall be burned up with fire. As for the flesh, everyone who is clean may eat flesh — a second category of peace offering disqualification: contact with impurity voids the meat even within the valid eating window. The principle that sacred meat must remain insulated from impurity sources creates a two-directional protection: the sacrificial meat protects the offerer through the atonement/fellowship mechanism; the community protects the sacred meat through purity maintenance.
20: But the person who eats of the flesh of the sacrifice of the LORD's peace offerings while an uncleanness is on him, that person shall be cut off (venikhreta) from his people — eating fellowship-offering meat while personally impure triggers karet; the offense is not merely eating tainted food but introducing personal impurity into the sacred communal feast. The severity of the penalty (karet vs. the guilt-bearing of v.18) reflects the distinction between consuming void meat (inadvertent offense) and deliberately eating sacred meat while unclean (presumptuous violation).
21: And if anyone touches an unclean thing, whether human uncleanness or an unclean beast or any unclean swarming thing, and then eats some flesh from the sacrifice of the LORD's peace offerings, that person shall be cut off from his people — the parallel to v.20 extends the karet penalty to anyone who has contracted impurity from any source (human, animal, insect) and then eats peace-offering meat; all three impurity categories of Lev 11–15 are named, creating comprehensive coverage.
22: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — a new revelation formula introduces the fat and blood prohibition (vv.23-27), which provides the legal basis for the brief statements in Lev 3:17 and 17:14.
23: Speak to the people of Israel, saying, You shall eat no fat of ox or sheep or goat — the fat prohibition is restricted to these three sacrificial species; the fat of game animals (deer, gazelle) may be eaten (Deut 12:15,22; 14:5), since game is never sacrificed. The restriction to sacrificial animals confirms that the fat prohibition is fundamentally about what belongs to YHWH in the cultic sphere, not a dietary rule about fat per se.
24: The fat of an animal that died of itself (neveilah) and the fat of one that was torn by beasts (terefah) may be put to any other use, but on no account shall you eat it — neveilah (carcass of an animal that died without proper slaughter) and terefah (torn by predator) fat may be used for other purposes (soap, lamp fuel) but not eaten; they are not sacrificeable, so their fat is not YHWH's altar portion, but the holiness-by-association principle keeps them from human consumption. The permitted non-food uses of this fat are consistent with ancient practices of animal rendering for industrial purposes.
25: For whoever eats of the fat of an animal of which a food offering may be made to YHWH shall be cut off from his people — consuming the sacrificial animals' fat (which belongs to YHWH) triggers karet; the fat reserved for the altar represents YHWH's proprietary claim over the life-sustaining richness of the sacrificial animal. Ancient Near Eastern texts describe gods as receiving the 'fat' of offerings (Gilgamesh Epic, Tablet XI: gods 'gathered like flies' over the sacrifice; the fat and smell are the divine portion), but only Israelite law makes human consumption of that fat a capital offense.
26: Moreover, you shall eat no blood whatever, whether of fowl or of animal, in any of your dwelling places (bechol moshevoteichem) — the blood prohibition extends to all locations (not only in the sanctuary), to all animal types (fowl and quadruped), and to all Israelites; this universality distinguishes the blood law from the fat law (which applies only to sacrificial species). In Lev 17:10-14 the theological basis is given: blood is the nefesh (life/soul) of the animal; consuming blood would be consuming life itself, which belongs to YHWH.
27: Whoever eats any blood, that person shall be cut off from his people — karet for blood consumption, as for fat (v.25); the equal penalty level confirms that blood and fat share the same cultic status as belonging definitively to YHWH rather than to human consumers. The blood prohibition is one of the most cross-culturally distinctive markers of Israelite identity in the ancient Near East; it is carried forward in the Jerusalem Council's ruling for Gentile believers (Acts 15:20,29).
28: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — a new revelation formula introduces the wave offering (tenupha) and heave offering (terumah) legislation for the peace offering (vv.29-36).
29: Speak to the people of Israel, saying, Whoever offers the sacrifice of his peace offerings to YHWH shall bring his offering to YHWH from the sacrifice of his peace offerings — the presentation ceremony for peace offerings begins with the offerer bringing specific portions to the priest; the sequence (offerer brings → priest waves/heaves → portions assigned) reflects the shared meal structure of the shelamim, in which YHWH, priest, and offerer all receive portions.
30: His own hands shall bring the LORD's food offerings. He shall bring the fat with the breast, that the breast may be waved as a wave offering (tenupha) before YHWH — the offerer personally presents the breast and fat together in the waving gesture (tenupha); the waving is a back-and-forth horizontal motion (rabbinic interpretation) that symbolizes presenting the offering to YHWH and receiving it back. The offerer's own hands perform the presentation, maintaining personal involvement even in an increasingly priestly-mediated system.
31: The priest shall burn the fat on the altar, but the breast shall be for Aaron and his sons — the fat burns on the altar (YHWH's portion); the breast (chazeh ha-tenupha, wave breast) becomes the collective priestly portion shared among all Aaron's sons (v.34). The distinction: fat → YHWH (altar); breast → all priests (shared); right thigh/shoulder → officiating priest (v.33).
32: And the right thigh (shoq ha-yamin) you shall give to the priest as a contribution (terumah) from the sacrifice of your peace offerings — the right thigh is a heave/contribution offering (terumah), elevated toward YHWH and then given to the officiating priest personally. The distinction between the wave breast (shared among all priests) and the heave thigh (given to the officiating priest) parallels the grain offering distinction of vv.9-10. The thigh/right side preference reflects the common ancient preference for the right side as the place of honor and power.
33: Whoever among the sons of Aaron offers the blood of the peace offerings and the fat shall have the right thigh for a portion — the blood-offering priest (who performed the blood manipulation) receives the right thigh; this ties the most valuable priestly portion to the most critical priestly action. The priest who bears the greatest ritual responsibility receives the most honorable perquisite.
34: For the breast that is waved and the thigh that is contributed I have taken from the people of Israel, out of the sacrifices of their peace offerings, and have given them to Aaron the priest and to his sons, as a perpetual due (chok-olam) from the people of Israel — the definitive legal authorization of the priestly food system: YHWH has 'taken' these portions from Israel and 'given' them to the priests as a permanent (olam) entitlement. The legal fiction of divine taking-and-giving grounds the priestly food system in YHWH's sovereign redistribution, not merely human convention.
35: This is the portion of Aaron and of his sons from the LORD's food offerings, from the day they were presented to serve as priests of YHWH — the closing formula traces the priestly portions to the ordination event (Lev 8–9), grounding the ongoing food-rights in the foundational consecration act. The mishchat aharon umishchat banav (the portion of Aaron and his sons) becomes the technical term for the priestly prebend system authorized here.
36: YHWH commanded this to be given them by the people of Israel, from the day that he anointed them. It is a perpetual due throughout their generations — the perpetuity formula (chok-olam ledorotam) for the priestly portions concludes the sacrificial law corpus; the same formula is used for the Sabbath (Exod 31:16-17) and for the Passover (Exod 12:17), placing the priestly support system in the same category of permanent covenantal obligations as the most foundational Israelite institutions.
37: This is the law of the burnt offering, of the grain offering, of the sin offering, of the guilt offering, of the ordination offering, and of the peace offering — the closing summary (zot ha-torah) names all six sacrifice types covered in Lev 6–7: olah, minchah, chatat, asham, millu'im (ordination), shelamim. The summary marks the completion of the priestly rubrics section begun at 6:8 and places all five regular sacrifice types plus the ordination offering under a single coherent legal framework.
38: Which YHWH commanded Moses on Mount Sinai, on the day that he commanded the people of Israel to bring their offerings to YHWH, in the wilderness of Sinai — the Sinaitic authority formula closes the entire corpus of sacrifice legislation (Lev 1–7): all the offering regulations originate at Sinai, in the wilderness, when YHWH commanded the offerings system into existence. The double location marker (Sinai mountain + Sinai wilderness) anchors the legislation in the specific historical moment of covenant formation.
